findings.message: The audit bundle still encodes ADR 1328 as requiring a separate `cli-presentation` layer/crate for rendering, and the same assumption is repeated in the README remediation text. That contradicts the ADR added in this PR, which explicitly rejects the `cli-presentation`/4-layer split and places render inside the new `cli_driver` primary adapter. Because this bundle is cited as the evidence/remediation base for the track, following it would steer the implementation toward an architecture the governing ADR rejects; update the findings/remediation wording to move rendering out of `cli_composition` into `cli_driver`, not `cli-presentation`.

The audit bundle is internally inconsistent about the category for `dry_results`: the README lists `apps/cli-composition/src/dry.rs:437-492` under `pre-formatted-stdout-stderr-in-composer-methods` and describes it as presentation/rendering work, but the source JSON records the same finding as `multi-step-orchestration` even though its own description also says it is output-formatting/presentation logic. This makes the category counts/remediation mapping unreliable for the plan artifact: downstream readers cannot tell whether this finding is part of the render migration or orchestration migration.

The plan artifacts give conflicting `CliApp` public-method counts for the same god-facade evidence. ADR 1328 uses `51 メソッド/27 ファイル` as the baseline, while the audit README says approximately 140 public methods and the JSON contains both `50 pub fn methods total` and `140+ pub fn methods`. Because this count is cited as evidence for D2/D6(e) decomposition scope, leaving all four values in the plan makes the evidence base self-contradictory and undermines sizing for the `CliApp` dissolution work.

The audit JSON has two category assignments swapped relative to the findings' own semantics and the README: `signal_check_gate` is labeled `pre-formatted-stdout-stderr-in-composer-methods` even though its rule/description and README section classify it as multi-step orchestration, while `dry_results` is labeled `multi-step-orchestration` even though its rule/description and README classify it as presentation formatting. This leaves the audit bundle internally contradictory while preserving the category counts, so ADR 1328's D1/D3/D4 evidence map can point readers at the wrong remediation bucket. Align these JSON categories with the README/description semantics.

D2 says `PrimaryAdapter` gets a `NoRoleInMethodSignature` check to keep domain/usecase role types out of public method signatures, and D3 separately lists `UseCase`, `Interactor`, `Command`, `Query`, and `ApplicationService` as forbidden in CLI layers. The rule specified here only forbids `Entity`, `AggregateRoot`, `ValueObject`, `Repository`, and `SecondaryPort`, so a `cli_driver` public method could still expose usecase command/query/interactor types without this signature rule firing. The lint plan does not enforce its own boundary-purity requirement unless those usecase roles are included or the decision is narrowed consistently.

`T014` creates and wires `cli_driver` while explicitly deferring the `architecture-rules.json` flip to `T021`, but CN-06 requires the crate creation and architecture-rules update to land in the same commit. As written, the staged rollout cannot satisfy its own gate, so the plan needs either a single atomic task/commit here or a relaxed constraint.

`FsArchivedTrackTelemetryAdapter` is described as having both the telemetry file path and clock source injected as private fields, but the paired task definition hardcodes `new(telemetry_dir: PathBuf) -> Self` and still calls `chrono::Utc::now()` directly. That leaves the telemetry abstraction internally inconsistent about whether time is injected or sourced directly, so the adapter contract is not coherent as written.

`T005`-`T008` add delegation shims in `cli_composition`, but `T021` only removes render helpers and shims pointing to infrastructure adapters. Nothing deletes the D4 usecase-delegation shims, so the final state can still contain `cli_composition` invoke calls and miss `AC-03`/`CN-02`.

`T009` requires `apps/cli/Cargo.toml` to stop listing `chrono`/`serde_json` immediately after that commit, but `S8`/`T021` says the Cargo/deny/architecture dependency-graph changes happen atomically in the final flip. As written, the manifest edit is either unscheduled or split across commits.

`T005` requires `SignalGateInteractor::new()` to take injected ports, but the type catalogue models `SignalGateInteractor` as a zero-argument constructor and simultaneously says it holds injected ports as private fields. That is a direct contradiction: as written, the application service cannot satisfy the DI requirement without another catalog edit.

T001 specifies `FsArchivedTrackTelemetryAdapter` as a unit struct while also requiring `new(telemetry_dir: PathBuf) -> Self` and `emit` to write telemetry to that configured location. A unit struct cannot retain the injected telemetry directory; the type catalogue also describes the directory as injected into the adapter, so the implementation plan is internally contradictory and would leave the D8 telemetry extraction unimplementable as written.

AC-04 requires `pub struct CliApp;` to be absent, and task-coverage marks AC-04 as covered only by T013, but T013 explicitly allows a remaining `CliApp` compatibility shim when usages are not gone. That leaves the plan able to declare AC-04 covered while the forbidden type still exists; make T013 unconditionally remove the shim or move/extend AC-04 coverage to the final flip task that actually verifies removal.

The D8 telemetry path is catalogued only as `ArchivedTrackTelemetryPort`, a SecondaryPort, while T009 says the archived-track path delegates persistence directly to the infrastructure adapter. There is no usecase ApplicationService/Interactor for the driver to invoke, which conflicts with the ADR/spec model that `cli_driver` invokes use cases while secondary adapters remain behind usecase services. Add a telemetry application service or otherwise make the plan explicit that the driver is not calling a secondary port directly.

T009 requires routing the archived-track path through `cli_driver` and testing driver dispatch to the telemetry service, but the same plan does not stage the telemetry driver until T016 and explicitly defers live `cli_driver` wiring/imports to T021. As written, this makes T009 unexecutable without either violating the later atomic cutover constraint or referencing a driver path that does not exist yet. Defer the driver-dispatch part to T016/T021, or move T009 after the driver exists.

The usecase catalogue only defines SignalGate, SpecAdrSignal, and ArchivedTrackTelemetry application services/interactors, while the implementation plan also requires T007/T008 to extract diff-fragment, dry_check/dry_write, fixpoint dry-gate branching, and PR polling into usecase functions/application services. Because `functions` is empty and no additional application-service entries exist for those planned D4 extractions, the type catalogue and implementation plan disagree about the public usecase surface needed to satisfy IN-07/AC-07.

`OS-03` is mapped to `T022`, but `T022` only updates `.harness/custom/review-prompts/*` and never touches `knowledge/conventions/hexagonal-architecture.md` (see `impl-plan.json:131-132`). The spec entry at `spec.md:46` says the convention-doc edit is deferred to a separate post-implementation task, so the task-coverage claim that all 4 out-of-scope items are covered is incorrect.

OS-01 is the out-of-scope boundary for downstream 1420 work, but its `adr_refs` point directly at ADR 1420 D1-D3 instead of the 1328 decision that defines this track boundary. That makes the 1328 plan artifact trace to downstream requirements as if they were source decisions for this track, contradicting the intended 1328-only provenance/no-1420-leakage for spec elements.

T007 tells implementers to add `D4OrchestrationError` as part of `usecase::dry_check::fragment_pipeline`, but the type catalogue defines that same type with `module_path: "d4_orchestration"` and the contract map renders it under `usecase::d4_orchestration`. This leaves two competing owning modules for a shared T007/T008 error type, so the implementation can follow the task text and drift from the catalogue. Align the task text or catalogue to a single module path.

`OS-03` is now mapped to an empty task list, leaving one of the four out-of-scope spec elements without any implementation-plan coverage. The Phase 3 task-coverage artifact is supposed to provide a reverse mapping for every spec element, so this contradicts the plan artifact coverage contract and will leave the rendered spec with no task anchor for that item.

The final flip section says AC-09 confirms “all 7 adapters relocated,” but the spec’s AC-09 and the D7 task breakdown define the intended end state as six adapters relocated to infrastructure and `LazyBranchReader` removed via UFCS/SystemGitRepo cleanup. Leaving T021 with the “all 7 relocated” condition contradicts the detailed acceptance criteria and can send implementers to relocate `LazyBranchReader` even though the plan elsewhere says it must be eliminated.

GO-04 says all seven port-implementing adapters are moved to `libs/infrastructure`, but the detailed scope and acceptance criteria later say `LazyBranchReader` is removed instead while only the other six adapters are relocated. This leaves the top-level goal contradicting the implementation plan and can misdirect implementers toward creating a forbidden/obsolete infrastructure adapter for `LazyBranchReader`.

This delta is no longer limited to the T002 catalogue/signal normalization described in the briefing: `impl-plan.json` advances task bookkeeping (`T001` -> `done`, `T002` -> `in_progress`) and `plan.md` increments the resolved count to `1/22`. The brief explicitly said this scope should not change `impl-plan.json`, so the artifact set now includes out-of-scope plan-state updates rather than only the adapter-catalogue/signal edits.

`RecordingDryAgent`'s catalogue docs still describe the private fields as `inner agent + telemetry writer`, but the same artifact now declares `RecordingDryAgent::new(inner: A) -> (Self, TieredDryAgentRecorder)`. That constructor shape no longer injects a telemetry writer and instead exposes the recorder pair, so the catalogue gives readers the old ownership model. Update the docs to describe the inner agent plus per-tier recorders / returned `TieredDryAgentRecorder`.

T005 still describes `SignalGateInteractor::new()` as taking only `SignalLayerReader` plus `SignalGateMatrix`, but the current signal-gate implementation now injects three additional chain-runner ports (`AdrChainRunnerPort`, `SpecAdrChainRunnerPort`, `LayerChainRunnerPort`). That leaves the plan artifact out of sync with the actual constructor contract and hides a significant dependency change.

The `SignalGateInteractor` catalogue entry still says it holds only `SignalLayerReader` and `SignalGateMatrix` as private fields. The current source stores three more ports (`adr_runner`, `spec_adr_runner`, `layer_chain_runner`), so the type catalogue no longer reflects the real dependency set.

`SpecAdrSignalInteractor` is still described as doing the full `read->decode->evaluate->set->write` cycle, but the updated `SpecFileWriterPort` in the same file now returns/accepts `domain::SpecDocument` and explicitly says JSON codec lives in the adapter. That makes the layer boundary inconsistent: either the interactor still owns decode/encode, or the port docs/signatures need to be updated to match the adapter-owned codec split.

`SpecAdrSignalService` still describes the application service as running a `read->decode->evaluate->set->write` cycle, but the same catalogue now defines `SpecFileWriterPort` as returning and accepting `domain::SpecDocument` with JSON decode/encode handled inside the infrastructure adapter. That leaves the plan contradictory about which layer owns codec work, and an implementer following the service/task text could put serde/codec behavior back into the usecase interactor despite the updated port contract.

The T006 plan-artifact delta is supposed to leave `impl-plan.json` byte-identical and avoid scope creep, but this change mutates task state for T005 by marking it `done` and adding a commit hash, with the rendered plan reflecting the same resolved-count change. That makes this catalogue/signal regeneration update carry unrelated task-status changes, so the plan artifacts no longer describe a clean T006-only delta.

`CodeFragmentExtractorPort::extract` takes `std::path::Path` by value in the catalogue, which is not a valid implementable Rust parameter type because `Path` is unsized. The same invalid signature is mirrored in the rendered markdown. This needs to be `&Path` or `PathBuf` before the port can be implemented as described.

T007 is internally inconsistent about `dry_write` record aggregation. The plan and the interactor/service docs say T007 moves `dry_write` metrics aggregation into `DryFragmentPipeline*`, but `DryFragmentPipelineOutput` says `records_before/after/appended` are always 0 in this service and that aggregation lives in the dry-write path. The ownership of those counters needs to be made consistent across the plan artifacts.

The catalogue says `DryFragmentPipelineService` covers the `dry_check_approved` fragment-ref derivation moved out of `cli_composition`, but the implementation still derives `current_fragment_refs` inline in `apps/cli-composition/src/dry.rs` and only delegates the fixpoint `build_current_fragment_refs` path. This makes the plan artifact semantically overstate the completed D4 extraction and can let the plan signal Blue for a responsibility that still remains in the CLI composition layer. Update the catalogue/plan wording to match the current source, or complete the `dry_check_approved` delegation before marking this coverage as moved.

`DryFragmentPipelineInteractor` still claims its private dependencies are only existing dry-check/diff ports and that no new infrastructure adapter edge is introduced, but the same catalogue now adds `CodeFragmentExtractorPort`, adds `CodeFragmentExtractorAdapter`, and declares the interactor constructor dependency on `Arc<dyn CodeFragmentExtractorPort>`. That is a semantic contradiction in the plan artifacts and can mislead the implementation boundary for T007; update this docs field to acknowledge the new fragment-extractor secondary port/adapter edge.

`DryFragmentPipelineCommand` still tells callers to set the CWD to `repo_root`, but the paired `DryCheckDiffSource` change explicitly removes the ambient CWD dependency by taking `repo_root` as an argument and calling `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(repo_root)`. Those requirements conflict: the command no longer needs the caller to `chdir` just to satisfy the diff source, so the command contract is stale and will mislead the T007/T008 handoff.

`DryCheckDiffSource` is modified for T007 and its docs say the adapter now uses `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(repo_root)`, but the scoped artifacts never catalogue the corresponding diff-source adapter or its `impl DryCheckDiffSource` edge. The infrastructure catalogue only adds `CodeFragmentExtractorAdapter`, and the contract map shows `DryCheckDiffSource` as an unattached secondary port. That leaves the T007 pipeline wiring semantically incomplete in the plan artifacts.

`DryCheckDiffSource` is added as the T007 modified diff-source port, but this catalogue entry omits the `module_path` and `spec_refs` that the neighboring T007 usecase entries have. The rendered artifacts therefore place it directly under `usecase`, while the adapter side says it implements `usecase::dry_check::ports::DryCheckDiffSource`, and `usecase-types.md` marks the same port Cat-Spec 🔴. That leaves the repo-root diff-source change inconsistently located and ungrounded in the plan artifacts.

T006 still describes `SpecAdrSignalInteractor` as owning the read→decode→evaluate→set→write cycle, but the updated port contract moved JSON decode/encode behind `SpecFileWriterPort` and now returns/accepts `SpecDocument`. That means the task text is pointing implementers at the wrong layer boundary.

`DryFragmentPipelineOutput` is documented as carrying `records_*` aggregate counts, but the same T007 plan says those fields are compatibility counters fixed at 0 and that the real aggregation stays in `dry_write`. The DTO contract is internally inconsistent about whether these fields are meaningful metrics or placeholders.

`FixpointDryGateOutput` is still labeled as a DTO, but it carries `Arc<dyn DryCheckApprovalService + Send + Sync>` in `dry_approval`. That turns the output into a live dependency bundle rather than data-only output and conflicts with the declared DTO role for this artifact.

The output-role classification is internally inconsistent for the new D4 outputs: `SignalGateOutput` is tagged as `UseCase` here even though its own docs describe it as an output DTO, while `FixpointDryGateOutput` is tagged as `Dto` even though its docs explicitly say it should be `UseCase` because it carries a live `DryCheckApprovalService`. The rendered `usecase-types.md` / `contract-map.md` mirror the same swap, so the artifact set currently disagrees about whether these two outputs are DTOs or use cases.

`FixpointDryGateOutput` is catalogued as `UseCase` even though this same artifact defines it as the return payload of `FixpointDryGateService::resolve_dry_gate` and documents it as an output bundle. That classifies a service output as a use-case role in the generated catalogue/contract map, so the plan artifacts will publish the wrong TDDD role for the new API.

`PrRepoNwoPort` is declared as a new blue secondary port even though the same catalogue says `repo_nwo` is pre-resolved by the caller and carried on `PrReviewPollingCommand`, and `PrReviewPollingInteractor::new` only injects reviews/reactions/comments/sleep ports. That makes the plan artifacts contradict the intended boundary for PR polling: the usecase contract now advertises a repository-resolution dependency that production explicitly does not use, and the generated markdown/contract map will preserve that false port as completed.

The catalogue says `FixpointDryGateInteractor` owns the CWD guard, and the plan repeats that `FixpointDryGateService` owns the CWD guard. A CWD guard is process-global `std::env` state management, while the same T008 boundary models diff-base/corpus work behind secondary ports specifically to keep the usecase interactor free of `std::env`/I/O. Following this artifact would put infrastructure orchestration back into the usecase layer and violate CN-07; the plan/catalogue should assign CWD guard ownership to the composition/infrastructure caller and leave the service with explicit `repo_root` inputs.

`FixpointDryGateOutput` is classified as `Dto` here, but its own docs say it is intentionally not a DTO and describe it as a usecase-owned bundle carrying a service dependency. The generated markdown still lists it under DTOs, so the artifact set is internally inconsistent about this type's layer/classification.

T009 is now marked in progress even though this same plan says T001 must be completed before the cli-bin I/O removal and that S4 depends on T001, while T001 remains only in progress. That makes the task ordering self-contradictory: the plan is directing work on T009 before its required telemetry service/adapter prerequisite is resolved.

T013 names `dry_fix_runner` as one of the Group 3 command families, but the task only requires `DryCompositionRoot`, `PrCompositionRoot`, `ReviewCompositionRoot`, and `SemanticDupCompositionRoot`. Because the surrounding S5 plan establishes one per-context `CompositionRoot` per command family, this leaves the dry-fix-runner family without an explicit root while still claiming all remaining `CliApp` command logic will be removed in T013.

`plan.md` still renders T017 as todo (`[ ]`), but `impl-plan.json` marks T017 as `in_progress`. That leaves the generated plan out of sync with the canonical task state and misstates the active work item.

`plan.md` now marks the track as 22/22 resolved, but the regenerated usecase type view still shows yellow implementation signals for `ArchivedTrackTelemetryPort` and `ArchivedTrackTelemetryCommand`. Yellow means the type goal is still in progress / must be resolved before track end, so this is a semantic contradiction in the plan artifacts: the track is presented as complete while the surviving type catalogue says part of the usecase contract is not fully resolved.

T023 says it is taking on the deferred T021 sub-step (5)+(6) work, but the same S10 section assigns sub-step (6), the cli_composition original/shim removal, to T024. That makes the task boundary contradictory: a reader cannot tell whether removal belongs in the in-progress T023 cutover or the follow-up T024 cleanup.

The infrastructure catalogue marks `FsGitWorkflowAdapter`, `FsRefVerifyAggregateAdapter`, `FsRefVerifyCheckApprovedAdapter`, and `FsRefVerifyRunAdapter` as secondary adapters, but their implemented traits are listed in `usecase-types.md` as application services rather than secondary ports. That contradicts the plan's hexagonal boundary: infrastructure adapters should implement secondary ports, while application services are usecase-layer behavior/interactors.

S8/T021 still records the completed final flip as having removed the original cli_composition render helpers and satisfied AC-06, but the new S10 section says T021 explicitly deferred that original-removal sweep and that AC-06 is only satisfied after S10. These are conflicting plan requirements/status claims, so readers cannot tell whether AC-06 is already complete or still pending under T024.

S8/T021 still states that T021 is the only commit touching the architecture/Cargo dependency graph, but T025 explicitly schedules removing the now-dead `chrono` and `serde_json` direct dependencies from `apps/cli/Cargo.toml`. That is another Cargo dependency-graph change after T021, so the plan contradicts its own CN-06/atomic-dependency-graph boundary. Move that manifest cleanup back into T021 or drop the uniqueness claim.

T023 says every `cli_driver::*Driver::handle` will invoke the corresponding usecase application service, but the regenerated catalogue places command-family boundaries such as `ArchPort`, `ConventionsPort`, `GitWorkflowService`, and `VerifyPort` under Secondary Ports instead of Application Services, with infrastructure adapters implementing them. That documents a driver path through infrastructure-backed secondary ports for those families, contradicting the T023 primary-adapter-to-usecase-service boundary.

T023/T024 plan the live path to move through cli_driver and then remove the cli_composition originals, but the regenerated catalogue still defines the new DryDriverPort as a secondary port implemented by a cli_composition adapter delegating to DryCompositionRoot / DryFixRunnerCompositionRoot. That makes the planned T024 removal infeasible for dry/dry_fix_runner unless the plan also replaces this old-root dependency with a real usecase service path before deleting those roots. SemanticDupDriverPort has the same pattern at usecase-types.json:3012.

S6/S7 still say the live wiring/import cutover and AC-06/AC-08 completion happen at T021, but S8/S10 now say T021 deferred that work and S10/T023-T025 complete it. That leaves the plan with conflicting acceptance boundaries for when driver wiring, render-helper removal, and thin-bin ACs are actually satisfied; update the earlier S6/S7 descriptions (and generated plan view) to point those completions at S10 instead of T021.

T023 now includes `dry_fix_runner` in the set of cli_driver families to cut over, but the preceding staging section says T019 was the last staged render family and only stages `dry` and `semantic_dup`. That leaves the plan claiming all driver render targets were already staged while T023 depends on a family that was never staged, making the implementation boundary for `dry_fix_runner` inconsistent.

`GitWorkflowService` is catalogued as an application service/primary port, but the infrastructure catalogue wires `FsGitWorkflowAdapter` as a secondary adapter implementing that same service directly. That lets T023 satisfy the driver cutover by injecting an infrastructure adapter as the usecase service, bypassing the usecase interactor boundary and contradicting the plan's own primary-adapter -> usecase -> secondary-adapter layering. The catalogue should instead put the filesystem operations behind a secondary port and have the usecase `GitWorkflowInteractor` implement the application service before the CLI driver calls it.

T023 requires every listed cli_driver family, including `domain` and `guard`, to be rewired through a corresponding catalogued usecase boundary trait, but the scoped usecase catalogue does not define any Domain* or Guard* port/service in either the Secondary Ports or Application Services tables. As written, the plan is not executable for those two driver families without adding the missing catalogue entries or narrowing T023's scope.

`FsGitWorkflowAdapter` is catalogued as an infrastructure secondary adapter implementing `GitWorkflowService`, while `GitWorkflowService` is catalogued as an Application Service in the usecase catalogue. That places a usecase application-service boundary directly in infrastructure rather than behind a secondary port, contradicting the layer roles the plan is supposed to enforce for the driver cutover.

T023 requires each `cli_driver::*Driver::handle` to call a usecase boundary trait/service, but several T023 command families are only catalogued as secondary outbound ports here (for example `ArchPort`, with the same pattern for conventions/file/git/verify and matching infrastructure adapters in `infrastructure-types.md`). Following these artifacts would make primary drivers call outbound infrastructure-port contracts directly, or leave those families without the usecase application-service boundary that T023 says the cutover must use. That is a semantic layer-placement contradiction in the plan.

`AC-06` and `AC-08` are still mapped only to `T014-T021` here, but the regenerated plan now says both acceptance criteria are only satisfied after `S10/T023-T025` ([plan.md:87-91](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/cli-composition-split-presentation-layer-2026-06-21/plan.md#L87), [impl-plan.json:137-149](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/cli-composition-split-presentation-layer-2026-06-21/impl-plan.json#L137)). The coverage matrix is now out of sync with the plan text.

`T025` still uses the example `usecase::pr_workflow::poll_codex_review`, but the updated usecase catalogue models the PR path as `usecase::pr::PrCommandService`/`PrCommandInteractor` and the review path as `usecase::review_v2::ReviewService` ([usecase-types.json:3147-3176](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/cli-composition-split-presentation-layer-2026-06-21/usecase-types.json#L3147), [usecase-types.json:3241-3268](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/cli-composition-split-presentation-layer-2026-06-21/usecase-types.json#L3241)). That module reference is stale relative to the regenerated catalogue and will misdirect the extraction.

AC-03 is still mapped only to T005-T008, T011-T013, and T021, but `plan.md` makes T024 the deferred deletion sweep for the remaining `cli_composition` originals/shims. As written in `task-coverage.json`, the acceptance gate can go green before the invoke-bearing source is actually removed.

AC-06 is broadened past its own wording: it still claims to measure only `cli_composition` presentation leaks, but `task-coverage.json` and `spec.md` add T025, which is a bin-only thin-bin cleanup in `apps/cli`. That makes the AC-06 mapping depend on work that never touches `cli_composition`.

`CN-08` is still covered only by T001/T009/T021, and `AC-10` has the same T001/T009/T021 coverage, but the plan now says T009 deliberately avoids live driver dispatch and T021 did not complete live wiring; S10/T023 is where the archived telemetry driver cutover happens. That leaves the artifacts claiming the driver-backed telemetry I/O requirement is satisfied by completed tasks even though the plan defers the required driver path to an in-progress task, so the coverage can close before the AC is actually planned to be met.

The briefing says `spec.json` should remain byte-identical with no AC/spec changes for this plan-artifacts round, but this change adds new in-scope requirements IN-13/IN-14/IN-15 here. That expands the behavioral contract for T023 instead of only adding S10 tasks/catalogue refreshes, so downstream coverage and review approval are now tied to requirements the round explicitly said not to introduce.

IN-13 describes `RefVerifyAggregateService` and `GitWorkflowService` as application-service traits, while the adjacent IN-14 entry and the generated usecase catalogue classify the same names as secondary ports. The plan now assigns the same boundary types to two different architectural roles, leaving T023 unclear on whether cli_driver should invoke application services/interactors or ports for these families.

AC-02 is mapped only to T014-T021, but T023 is the task that changes `cli_driver::*Driver::handle` from staged/stubbed drivers to injected usecase invocation with no direct infrastructure imports. That contradicts the S10/T023 plan text and lets the driver-handle acceptance criterion be treated as covered before the actual live handle cutover is linked.

CN-08 forbids direct bin I/O including `serde_json`/`chrono`, but its task coverage omits T025 even though T025 explicitly removes remaining `chrono` + `serde_json` bin business logic. As written, CN-08 can be considered covered after T023 while the plan still says direct thin-bin cleanup remains in T025.

AC-13 still covers only T001-T022, but S10 adds T023/T024/T025 as remaining track tasks and T023/T024 both state CI must stay green. This leaves the final CI acceptance criterion disconnected from the new S10 cutover/removal work, contradicting the plan's claim that S10 completes the deferred live wiring and cleanup.

T023 permits cli_driver handlers to invoke SecondaryPort traits directly for pass-through families. That contradicts the spec/ADR model where cli_composition wires secondary adapter -> usecase interactor/application service -> driving adapter, and the primary adapter invokes a use case rather than a driven port. Leaving this wording gives the implementation plan permission to bypass the application-service boundary for these families, which weakens the intended layer split.

The new S10 tasks are not tied back to the in-scope requirements they implement. For example, T023 is the live Driver::handle cutover, but IN-01/IN-02/IN-06 coverage still lists only the earlier staging/final-flip tasks, so the traceability now says the new live cutover work is not part of the in-scope contract. Add the S10 tasks to the relevant in_scope rows or restore explicit in-scope items for this work.

T024 removes the `tee_stderr_to_file` shim from `cli_composition`, but the only fallback it names is to re-export that infrastructure helper from `cli_driver` or inline it at the bin use site. Both choices conflict with the declared layer rules (`cli_driver` may depend only on `usecase`, and `apps/cli` must stay thin), so this cleanup task has no legal owner for the helper if it is still needed.

The new usecase catalogue still places presentation/formatting APIs in `usecase`: `ArchPort` exposes `render_*`, and telemetry adds `TelemetryAggregateService.report` plus the `usecase::telemetry::format_report` free function. That conflicts with IN-06/AC-02, which require invoke+render to live in `cli_driver` rather than in usecase.

T023 says every listed family should be wired through an injected usecase service/interactor, but the catalogue updates only add secondary ports for `ArchPort`, `ConventionsPort`, and `VerifyPort` and do not add matching application-service/interactor entries for those families. That leaves `cli_driver` without a concrete usecase boundary to inject for part of the cutover, so the task is under-specified/infeasible as written.

`TelemetryAggregateService` is introduced as the telemetry driver-facing application service, but the reviewed artefacts only record `TelemetryReportInteractor -> TelemetryReportService`. There is no implementation edge for `TelemetryAggregateService`, so the T023 telemetry wiring described in the plan has no recorded provider and cannot be completed as written.

The S10 tasks are not wired into the constraint coverage even though the plan assigns those responsibilities to S10. For example, T023 explicitly owns the live `cli_driver` cutover with no direct infrastructure imports, but `CN-01` still stops at T021 here; similarly the pure-composition and thin-bin constraints remain tied only to the pre-S10 tasks while T023-T025 are the tasks that now complete that work. This makes the task coverage contradict the updated spec/plan and can let S10 proceed without its core architectural constraints attached to the new tasks.

T023 says every `cli_driver` family, including git/ref_verify, must call an injected usecase application/command-service and must not invoke secondary ports directly. The refreshed catalogue instead classifies `GitWorkflowService`, `RefVerifyAggregateService`, `RefVerifyCheckApprovedDriverService`, and `RefVerifyRunService` as `secondary_port`, with infrastructure adapters implementing those same traits. This path should delegate through an injected service/driver boundary so the composition root only constructs the object graph (ADR D2, hexagonal-architecture.md §CLI as Composition Root).

`GitInput::SwitchAndPull` is still routed through `handle`, but this T021 stub fabricates an `[OK]` switch/pull message and returns `CommandOutcome::success(Some(stdout))` without invoking `GitWorkflowInteractor`. The briefing requires every handle-reachable stub to fail loudly with the transitional `CommandOutcome::failure(...)` message until cli_composition cutover; accidental callers of this mutating path would still report success while doing no git work.

Public `Default` impls in `apps/cli-driver/src/guard.rs:77`, `apps/cli-driver/src/signal.rs:194`, and `apps/cli-driver/src/track.rs:221` still instantiate stub services with `Arc::new(...)`. That keeps object-graph construction inside `cli_driver` instead of the `cli_composition` wiring layer, so the injected-side boundary is not actually enforced.

ReviewDriver now owns eleven separate use-case service handles and `handle` dispatches between them by `ReviewInput` variant. Under the cli_driver D3/D4 policy, multi-interactor operation selection belongs behind a usecase application service, while the primary adapter should translate input, invoke one injected interactor, and render. Collapse these review operations behind a single injected review service or split the driver per use case.
findings.category: handle not delegating to single use case

findings.message: RefVerifyDriver injects both `RefVerifyRunService` and `RefVerifyCheckApprovedDriverService`, then `handle` branches between them at runtime. That keeps multi-usecase orchestration in cli_driver instead of usecase/composition, violating the scope policy that a Driver delegates a request to a single injected interactor.
findings.category: handle not delegating to single use case

findings.message: TelemetryDriver injects a report service and a separate emit port, and `handle` selects between those collaborators by input variant. Per the cli_driver adapter policy, this branching between separate interactors should be hidden behind one usecase application service or separated into distinct drivers so the adapter remains input -> invoke -> render.
findings.category: handle not delegating to single use case

findings.message: The skill-compliance hook path bypasses the injected `HookDispatchService` and implements prompt classification plus direct `usecase::skill_compliance` calls inside a helper called by `handle`. This is business logic and alternate usecase orchestration in the primary adapter; move the skill-compliance decision/rendering behind an injected usecase service so `HookDriver` only translates input, invokes one interactor, and renders.

`review_run_fix_local` now writes the raw status (`completed` / `blocked_cross_scope` / `failed`) to stdout, but the fixer protocol in the composition layer emits `REVIEW_FIX_STATUS: <status>`. This strips the sentinel that downstream tooling parses and changes the observable CLI contract.

`track_id: None` is no longer auto-resolved here: `review_check_approved` (and the same pattern again in `review_persist_commit_hash`) collapses `None` to `""` before calling the service. The composition path resolves the current branch instead, so the documented branch-detection behavior is now broken and these commands fail validation on omitted track IDs.

`HookDriver::hook_dispatch` makes runtime hook-policy decisions before invoking the injected service: `skill-compliance` is routed to a separate service method and `git-ref-update` final notifications return success without calling `HookDispatchService::dispatch`. That violates the cli_driver rule that a Driver request should be input -> one interactor -> render, and it bypasses usecase guard logic for those hook invocations. Move this routing/allow decision behind the injected usecase service.
findings.category: handle not delegating to single use case

findings.message: `DomainDriver::domain_export_schema` performs command side effects after the usecase returns by writing `input.output` directly with `std::fs::write`. Under the cli_driver contract, the driver should translate input, invoke the injected usecase/port, then render a `CommandOutcome`; file output behavior belongs behind the usecase boundary or an injected port rather than in the primary adapter.

`review_run_codex` and `review_run_claude` build and validate the base reviewer prompt in the driver, then pass `briefing_file: None` to `ReviewService`. That helper performs file existence validation and prompt construction in cli_driver instead of leaving the raw `briefing_file`/`prompt` command to the injected usecase service, so the adapter is no longer just input -> invoke -> render.

`HookDriver::hook_dispatch` is doing hook-protocol validation and hook-name-specific command construction before invoking the injected service. The extra-argument check returns a `CommandOutcome` without calling `HookDispatchService`, and the same helper then branches on `git-ref-update`/`git-pre-push`/`skill-compliance` to decide how to parse stdin and build the command. Per the cli_driver D3/D4 policy, adapter helpers should stay to input -> invoke -> render; these validation and hook-domain decisions should live behind the usecase service so the primary adapter always delegates one request to the injected interactor.

The archived-track integration test no longer proves the fix: `assert!(duration_ms < 60_000)` still passes when `duration_ms` is 0, so a regression that drops duration propagation would remain undetected. The assertion should explicitly reject zero (or otherwise check that the duration was actually recorded).

These track CLI helpers now always return `Ok(ExitCode::from(outcome.exit_code))` after `driver.handle(...)`, even when the driver reports a failure. That means `dispatch_track_cmd`/`execute_with_error_chain()` will treat failing track subcommands as success paths, so the structured error chain and `NonZeroExit.error_chain` telemetry are dropped. Preserve an `Err(CliError::Message(...))` on driver failures or change the dispatcher to consume the outcome directly.

PR command dispatch still calls legacy `PrCompositionRoot` methods such as `app.pr_push(...)` instead of obtaining `pr_driver()` and calling `handle(PrInput::...)`. That leaves the bin layer on the old composition API after the call-site flip and violates the ADR D5 thin-bin driver boundary.

Review local dispatch still bypasses `ReviewDriver` by calling `ReviewCompositionRoot::new().review_run_codex(input)` directly. The review driver already exposes `ReviewInput::RunCodex`, so this command remains outside the required parse -> driver.handle(input) boundary from ADR D5.

Verify dispatch still routes every subcommand through direct `VerifyCompositionRoot` methods like `app.verify_tech_stack(...)` rather than `verify_driver().handle(VerifyInput::...)`. This preserves business dispatch in the bin layer and misses the T023 driver-coordinate flip required by ADR D5.

Track branch handlers still call `track_branch_create` / `track_branch_switch` directly and emit stdout in the command handler. `TrackInput::BranchCreate` and `TrackInput::BranchSwitch` exist, so these handlers should delegate through `track_driver().handle(...)` and central outcome emission per ADR D5/D8.

`execute_find_similar` and `execute_dup_check` still perform application-level work in the CLI before dispatching: `execute_find_similar` reads `--file` contents into `fragment_text`, while `execute_dup_check` opens `--files-from`, parses lines, filters empties, and converts them to paths before calling `semantic_dup_driver().handle(...)`. Under the CLI thin-bin policy, handlers should only parse arguments into typed driver input and emit the resulting `CommandOutcome`; file I/O and input normalization belong behind the driver/usecase boundary. This violates ADR D5/D8 and the CLI-as-composition-root rule.

`file write-atomic` still reads the application payload from `stdin` and enforces the byte limit inside the bin handler before dispatching to `FileDriver`. Under ADR D5/D8 and the CLI scope policy, `apps/cli` should only parse CLI args, obtain the composition-root driver, call `driver.handle(input)`, and emit the `CommandOutcome`; this stdin I/O/input validation needs to move behind the driver/usecase boundary.

`chrono` and `serde_json` were moved out of the bin crate's normal dependencies, but `apps/cli/src/commands/pr.rs` still uses both in production code (`serde_json::Value`/`serde_json::to_string` and `chrono::DateTime` parsing). That makes the `sotp` binary fail to compile until `pr.rs` is also extracted or these crates stay in `[dependencies]`.

`emit` creates and opens the telemetry path with plain `create_dir_all`/`OpenOptions::open`, so a symlinked `telemetry_dir` component or pre-existing `telemetry.jsonl` symlink is followed instead of being rejected. This adapter is the infrastructure I/O boundary for the injected telemetry path; when it is wired to archived-track logs, a symlink under the trusted archive tree can redirect appends outside that tree instead of failing closed. Match the existing CN-04/scope_config_loader symlink-rejection pattern, or use a trusted-root guard plus no-follow open before writing.

Path containment is never enforced after deriving `canonical_root`, so a crafted `items_dir` like `../other/track/items` can escape the intended trusted root before `review.json` / `.commit_hash` are opened. `reject_symlinks_below` only rejects symlinks; it does not replace the `canonical_path.starts_with(&canonical_root)` check used in `scope_config_loader.rs`.

The same trusted-root gap exists in the ref-verify adapter: `items_dir_abs` is built and used without proving it stays under `canonical_root`, so `RefVerifyScopeResolver` / `RefVerifyCacheAdapter` can be pointed at an escaped directory tree via a relative `items_dir`.

`review_status` no longer verifies that `track/items/<track-id>` exists before reading `review.json` and `.commit_hash`. Because both stores treat missing files as empty/`None`, a missing or mistyped track directory can now fall through to `Approved`/`ApprovedWithBypass` instead of failing like the original `build_v2_shared` path. Restore the `track_dir.is_dir()` guard here.

`resolve_gate_path_context()` anchors git discovery to `items_dir` itself for absolute paths. If that path sits under a nested checkout, `SystemGitRepo::discover_from(items_dir)` can select the nested repo as the trusted root, so these adapters read `review-scope.json`, `review.json`, `.commit_hash`, and ref-verify caches from the wrong repository instead of the caller's repo root. Anchor discovery to the caller's repo root first, as the composition layer did.

`resolve_gate_path_context()` anchors git discovery on `SystemGitRepo::discover()` from the process cwd, so the gate adapters no longer root `.commit_hash` ancestry checks in the validated `items_dir`/`canonical_root`. If the command runs from another repo, or from outside a repo, `FsCommitHashStore::read()` can consult the wrong repository or fail for a valid project. The previous implementation wrapped the same read in `with_repo_cwd(&canonical_root, ...)` to keep this I/O bounded to the trusted root.

`items_dir_abs.canonicalize()` follows a symlinked `items_dir` root, but this helper never rejects that root with a `symlink_metadata().file_type().is_symlink()` check. Other infrastructure adapters explicitly refuse to use a symlinked `items_dir` before treating it as the trusted anchor, because `reject_symlinks_below()` only protects descendants. As written, a symlinked `items_dir` can become the gate's trust root.

`resolve_gate_path_context` only rejects a symlink when the leaf `items_dir` path itself is a symlink. A symlinked ancestor (for example `track/` under the caller repo) can still redirect the canonicalized path into a different subtree, and the adapter will then treat that subtree as the project root. This should reject symlinks along the full path below the caller's trusted root, matching the repo's existing `reject_symlinks_below` boundary checks.

The `absolute items_dir` branch stops anchoring to the current repo: any absolute path outside the caller repo is accepted as long as its derived project root is a git root. That means the adapter can be pointed at a different repository instead of failing closed under the caller's trusted root.

`reject_symlinks_below()` is only applied when `items_dir_abs.starts_with(&canonical_repo_root)`, so an absolute `items_dir` that reaches the repo through a symlink outside the repo can bypass the symlink check and still be accepted after `canonicalize()`. The repo’s own loader pattern (`scope_config_loader.rs`) rejects symlinks on the original path unconditionally before trusting the result.

`FsReviewGateStateAdapter::review_status` only re-anchors the `.commit_hash` lookup; the subsequent `ReviewCycle::evaluate_approval` still runs with the caller's ambient CWD, but `GitDiffGetter` and `SystemReviewHasher` discover the repo from that CWD. In nested-checkout or subdirectory invocations this can make the adapter read diff/hash data from the wrong repository instead of the validated `canonical_root`. Anchor the whole evaluation on `canonical_root` (the way `build_v2_shared` does) before calling `evaluate_approval`.

`OpenOptions::open(&lock_path)` will follow an existing `<db_path>.lock` symlink, so a caller can end up locking an attacker-controlled target outside the intended index sidecar. This is an infrastructure I/O boundary; add a `symlink_metadata(&lock_path)` check before opening and reject `file_type().is_symlink()` like the existing real-file boundary checks.

`read_spec_json` reads the caller-supplied `spec.json` with `std::fs::read_to_string` directly, so a symlink at the file or any parent component is followed before decoding. `spec.json` has existing real-file semantics in the repo (`FsSpecFileLoader` and `verify/spec_states.rs` reject symlinks before reads, matching the symlink-rejection boundary pattern covered by `scope_config_loader.rs` tests). This new adapter reintroduces an unguarded spec load path for `signal calc-spec-adr`; add the same `reject_symlinks_below`/trusted-root guard before reading.

`CodeFragmentExtractorAdapter::extract` only delegates to `extract_code_fragments` and never applies the CWD guard that the T007 briefing assigns to this adapter. The caller path now restores CWD before invoking the fragment pipeline, while the diff side still relies on `SystemGitRepo::discover()` being rooted at `repo_root`; with this implementation, discovery depends on the ambient process CWD and can diff the wrong repo or fail in nested/monorepo layouts. The adapter/port boundary needs to carry `repo_root` and restore the original CWD on all paths, or the CWD guard must remain at the caller.

`DryCorpusRootManifest` is a versioned on-disk DTO but it is deserialized directly without `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]` or a schema-version envelope. A future `dry-check-corpus-root.json` with `schema_version: 1` plus new fields would be accepted and those fields silently ignored instead of failing closed, which is a serde codec regression in this layer.

`validate_workspace_root()` canonicalizes `workspace_root` and only checks that the resolved path stays under `repo_root`; it never rejects a symlinked root itself. That lets a manifest point `workspace_root` at a symlink inside the repo and have `compute_corpus_fingerprint()` traverse the symlink target, so the fingerprint can describe a different tree than the manifest records. Existing root-anchored adapters in this crate reject symlinked roots before canonicalization, so this boundary should do the same.

`compute_corpus_fingerprint` hashes paths relative to the root you pass in, so using `workspace_root` here drops the repo-relative prefix that `dry write` includes when it stores the corpus fingerprint. For any scoped workspace (for example `scoped/` under the repo root), the approval path will compute a different fingerprint from the one written to coverage and will incorrectly treat the corpus as stale.

`stage_from_file` resolves the caller-provided file path through `repo.resolve_path`, which accepts absolute paths and otherwise does `repo.root().join(path)` without rejecting `..` or checking the resolved path stays under the repo root. The resolved path is then read and, with `cleanup`, removed; the same pattern is repeated for commit/note files. This violates the trusted-root/CN-04 fail-closed pattern (`is_safe_briefing_path` rejects absolute and parent traversal) and lets these guarded git adapters operate on files outside the trusted repository root.

`track_id` is joined directly into `repo_root/track/archive/<track_id>/logs` before any domain validation or canonical under-root check. Unlike the telemetry report path, this emit path does not call `TrackId::try_new`; a malicious or accidentally unvalidated value containing traversal components can escape `track/archive` and append telemetry under another repo path. Validate the track id and/or canonicalize the final directory and fail closed unless it remains below the trusted archive root.

`add_convention_doc()`/`update_convention_index()` build and write `knowledge/conventions/README.md` and `knowledge/conventions/<slug>.md` via raw joined paths with no symlink check. A symlinked conventions dir or README can redirect the write outside the repo, so this needs the same fail-closed boundary guard used elsewhere.

`load_rules()` reads `architecture-rules.json` with plain `read_to_string` after `root.join(...)`, but never rejects a symlinked leaf. The TDDD loader already treats a symlinked `architecture-rules.json` as invalid; this path will accept one and run workspace verification against attacker-controlled JSON.

`load_ref_verify_config()` reads `.harness/config/ref-verify.json` with `read_to_string` after `try_exists()` only, so a symlinked config file is followed instead of rejected. The same adapter later loads `agent-profiles.json` the same way, so ref-verify behavior can be sourced from outside the trusted workspace.

P1 trusted-root violation: `load_rules()` now rejects symlinks below `root`, but `root` itself is still taken as trusted without any `symlink_metadata`/`canonicalize` check. Callers pass raw `project_root`, so a symlinked workspace root bypasses the new guard and can redirect reads of `architecture-rules.json` outside the intended tree. This is the same fail-closed root-validation pattern used in `verify/adr_signals.rs` before `reject_symlinks_below` is applied.

P1 trusted-root violation: the new convention-doc guards only validate descendants of `root`, but `root` itself is never verified before `join`/`create_dir_all`/`read_to_string`. If `project_root` is a symlink, `add_convention_doc()`, `update_convention_index()`, and `verify_convention_index()` still operate through that symlinked root and can read/write `knowledge/conventions/*` outside the intended tree. The root itself needs the same fail-closed validation used in other symlink-guarded loaders.

`resolve_repo_file_path()` validates the joined path, but it never verifies that `repo.root()` itself is a non-symlink trusted root before passing it to `reject_symlinks_below()`. That guard trusts `trusted_root`, so a symlinked checkout root can bypass the containment check. Add the same root check used in `arch.rs` / `conventions.rs` before joining user-supplied file paths.

`FsTelemetryEmitDynamicAdapter::emit_archived()` uses `repo.root()` as the trusted root for the telemetry archive path without first rejecting a symlinked repository root. `reject_symlinks_below()` will then trust that root and can be pointed outside the intended archive tree. Canonicalize or symlink-reject the root before deriving `archive_root`.

`resolve_project_root()` accepts a caller-supplied `<project_root>/track/items` path and `run()` canonicalizes it before any symlink/root validation. A symlinked `items_dir` can therefore redirect `discover_from()` and the subsequent config/profile/cache reads into a different repository. The same missing root guard is repeated in `FsTelemetryEmitDynamicAdapter` (`libs/infrastructure/src/telemetry/report_adapter.rs:133-153`). Fail closed on symlinked roots here, like the new `ensure_trusted_root` / `guard_convention_root` checks elsewhere in this PR.

`FsVerifyAdapter` forwards caller-controlled `project_root` directly into verify helpers that do `root.join(...)` / `read_dir(...)` (`verify_tech_stack`, `verify_latest_track`, `verify_layers`, `verify_hooks_path`, `verify_doc_links`, `verify_view_freshness`, etc.) without first rejecting a symlinked root. That makes the adapter trust a root that `reject_symlinks_below` would otherwise treat as inherently safe, so a symlinked workspace can redirect reads outside the repo. Add a fail-closed root check here, following the `verify::trusted_root::ensure_not_symlink_root` pattern used elsewhere.

`FsTelemetryReportAdapter` constructs `TelemetryReport` from `items_dir` without validating that `items_dir` itself is a non-symlink root. `TelemetryReport::aggregate()` only guards descendants below the supplied root, so a symlinked `items_dir` bypasses the guard and can redirect telemetry reads outside the workspace. Reject the root first (same fail-closed trusted-root pattern as other infrastructure entry points) before calling `TelemetryReport::new`.

`ensure_trusted_items_dir_root()` only checks the leaf `items_dir` path. `FsTelemetryReportAdapter::aggregate()` then treats that path as the trusted root for `TelemetryReport::aggregate()`, so a caller can point `items_dir` at a path with a symlinked ancestor and the later `reject_symlinks_below(..., items_dir)` checks will read telemetry outside the intended workspace. This needs a fail-closed containment check, not a leaf-only lstat.

`verify_spec_attribution` delegates a caller-supplied `spec_path` directly to the spec verifier without first guarding the sibling `spec.json` path. The delegated verifier checks `spec_json_path.is_file()` and then reads it with `std::fs::read_to_string`, both of which follow symlinks; unlike the existing trusted-root/symlink-guard pattern used for legacy spec reads, a `spec.json` symlink can be accepted and verified as if it were a real workspace file. Add a trusted-root containment check plus `reject_symlinks_below` before taking the spec.json path, and apply the same boundary guard to the sibling-spec paths used by the frontmatter/signals methods.

`collect_track_branch_claims` accepts directory entries with `entry.path().is_dir()`, which follows symlinks, and then reads `metadata.json` without a `reject_symlinks_below` check. Since `workflow_adapter` feeds these claims into the branch guard, a symlinked `track/items/<id>` or `track/archive/<id>` can make the adapter trust metadata outside the repo. This should follow the `scope_config_loader.rs` symlink-rejection pattern before accepting the entry and before reading metadata.

The persistent index lock path only rejects the lock file itself as a symlink after `create_dir_all(parent)`, but it never rejects symlinked parent components. A symlinked cache directory can redirect lock creation/opening outside the intended index location, so the lock may not protect the real index and the adapter writes through a symlinked boundary. Add an ancestor symlink guard using a trusted root, matching the repository symlink-rejection policy.

`resolve_project_root` accepts any `items_dir` whose last two components are `track/items`, but it never rejects `..`/absolute escapes or verifies the derived root stays under a trusted repository root. A caller can point this adapter at an unrelated tree and have it load config/profiles from there; this needs the same fail-closed containment check pattern as `is_safe_briefing_path`/CN-04.

`resolve_project_root_from_items_dir` does the same lexical parent/parent check for telemetry and then canonicalizes later, but it never proves the derived `project_root` is still under the intended repository root. A path like `../other/track/items` can escape the workspace before telemetry files are loaded; this should fail closed on traversal before any I/O.

`aggregate` resolves `items_dir` with `current_dir().join(items_dir)` and then only checks for symlinks under `/`. That still accepts `../` traversal, so a caller can point the telemetry report adapter at an arbitrary directory outside the repo instead of failing closed.

`acquire_persistent_index_lock` derives `<db_path>.lock` from untrusted input before any containment check, and `reject_lock_path_symlinks` uses `""`/`/` as the trusted root. A relative `../...` `db_path` can therefore create and lock files outside the intended cache root.

`load_explicit_track_branch_from_items_dir` canonicalizes caller-supplied `items_dir` and `track_dir`, but only checks that the track dir's parent equals the canonical items dir; it never verifies either resolved path stays under the trusted `root`. A caller can pass an absolute outside `items_dir` plus matching outside `track_dir`, and the adapter will read that external `metadata.json` despite the symlink checks. This should follow the CN-04/is_safe_briefing_path pattern and fail closed unless the resolved paths start under `root` before `read_metadata`.

`aggregate` still accepts an absolute `items_dir` outside the repository: `ensure_trusted_items_dir_root` keeps absolute paths as-is and then calls the symlink guard with `/` as the trusted root, so there is no canonical `starts_with(repo_root)` containment check before `TelemetryReport::new(items_dir)` reads `<items_dir>/<track_id>/logs/telemetry.jsonl`. That leaves `sotp telemetry report --items-dir /some/outside/tree` outside the CN-04 fail-closed trusted-root pattern used by `is_safe_briefing_path` and by the `emit_archived` path in this same adapter. Resolve the supplied path against the current repo root and reject absolute/out-of-root paths before constructing the report reader.

`verify_catalogue_spec_refs` only checks whether `items_dir` itself is a symlink before handing it to the catalogue verifier. It does not reject symlinked ancestors below `workspace_root` with `reject_symlinks_below(items_dir, workspace_root)`, so a path like `<repo>/track/items` can pass when `<repo>/track` is a symlink into another in-repo tree; the downstream reads then follow that symlink while using `items_dir` as the trusted root. This violates the infrastructure symlink-rejection boundary policy; mirror the existing scope-config-loader pattern by rejecting symlinks on every component under the trusted workspace root before delegation.

`derive_current_refs` still relies on the caller having mutated the process CWD before invocation, even though `repo_root` is carried in the command and the diff-source port does not receive it. That makes this usecase flow depend on ambient process state through `DryCheckDiffSource`/`GitDryCheckDiffGetter::discover()`: a caller with a different CWD, or concurrent code changing CWD, can list hunks from the wrong repository while fragments are normalized against `cmd.repo_root`. This is an implicit process dependency in the usecase orchestration boundary; bind the diff source to `repo_root` explicitly or pass the root through the port instead of relying on global CWD.

`PrReviewPollingService::poll` now has two new externally visible outcomes (`ZeroFindings` and exact-HEAD timeout recovery), but the added tests only cover `ReviewFound`, `Timeout`, and sleep cadence. There is no coverage for either `ZeroFindings` branch or the recovery path, so regressions in the main zero-findings logic can still ship unnoticed.

`resolve_dry_gate` now maps fragment-pipeline failures into `D4OrchestrationError::DryGate` at lines 596-599, but the test block only covers the enabled/disabled paths plus diff-base and corpus-meta failures. There is no assertion that a `fragment_pipeline` error propagates, so this new failure branch can regress unnoticed.

`PrReviewPollingService::poll` exposes several new `D4OrchestrationError::PrPolling` error paths (invalid trigger timestamp plus review/reaction/comment list and JSON parse failures), but the added tests only cover the success outcomes (`ReviewFound`, `ZeroFindings`, `Timeout`, recovery, and sleep cadence). None of the `Err` branches are asserted, so the new error mapping can regress without a failing test.

`poll` no longer enforces a wall-clock deadline; it stops after a fixed `max_iterations` count, so a slow GitHub API call can push the loop well past the caller's timeout. The original `Instant`-based check would have broken out once the deadline expired.

[libs/usecase/src/pr_review.rs:465-470,591] `poll` still reads wall-clock time inside the usecase via `Instant::now()` to derive and re-check the deadline. That makes timeout behavior depend on runtime time instead of an injected port or parameter, which breaks the usecase purity boundary and leaves the polling loop non-deterministic to test.

`RefVerifyDriverError` exposes infrastructure concerns (`Infrastructure`, `ConfigLoad`) as usecase-layer error variants. This makes the driver-facing usecase port aware of file/git/config failure categories instead of a usecase-level failure concept, violating the scope policy's error-type abstraction rule. Map adapter failures to usecase-level errors or keep them opaque at the port boundary.

`TelemetryReportError::Io` leaks an I/O failure category through the usecase telemetry report port. The usecase boundary should not require callers to know that report aggregation reads files; map this to a usecase-level concept such as report unavailable/read failed, or keep the infrastructure detail inside the adapter.

`RefVerifyDriverError` still exposes infrastructure-oriented constructors (`Infrastructure`, `ConfigLoad`) as public associated functions on the usecase error type. Even though the enum variants were renamed, adapters and callers can still depend on infrastructure/config failure categories through the usecase API instead of mapping them to the usecase-level `Unavailable` concept at the boundary.

`TelemetryReportError::Io` remains available as a public constructor on the usecase error type. That preserves the I/O-specific failure category in the usecase API, so callers can still know/report that aggregation failed due to file I/O instead of only seeing the usecase-level `ReportUnavailable` concept.

`ArchivedTrackTelemetryError` is still part of the public usecase API and exposes storage/serialization failure categories (`Io` and `Serialize`). Callers of `ArchivedTrackTelemetryService::emit` must therefore know this use case writes files and serializes JSON, which violates the usecase severity policy's error-type abstraction rule. Map these adapter failures to usecase-level concepts before returning across the boundary.

`ExportSchemaInteractor::export` now performs the requested schema output write itself by calling the injected `FileWritePort` when `output_path` is set. The usecase scope policy treats file writes as output side effects that belong in the CLI mapping layer, even when routed through a port; this makes the export interactor responsible for runtime output instead of only returning the exported JSON for the driver/CLI to render or persist.

`/track:dry-check` now introduces a `skipped` terminal state at `.claude/commands/track/dry-check.md:15`, but the top-level orchestrator still documents only `completed` / `blocked` / `failed` for the DFP step in `.claude/commands/track/full-cycle.md:43-52`. That leaves the new opt-out outcome unhandled on the main `/track:full-cycle` path, so repositories with `dry-check.enabled: false` can still stall before Review instead of flowing through as intended.

Step 0a now short-circuits before the existing `track/<id>` branch check in Step 0b, so when `.harness/config/dry-check.json` is missing or `enabled:false`, `/track:dry-check` can return success even from a non-track branch. That bypasses the command's own branch guard and can let `/track:full-cycle` continue from the wrong workspace.

This reviewer prompt is part of the template distribution surface, but it tells future reviewers to cite "AC-05 of the track spec" as authority for the `Result<_, String>` rule. That acceptance criterion belongs to the current implementation track, not a durable ADR/convention file present in a fresh consumer checkout, so the shipped policy will contain an unresolvable reference. Replace it with a stable citation such as ADR D2 or state the typed-error rule directly.

The new `cli_driver` review prompt declares policy for `apps/cli-driver/`, but the scope loader is not wired to it: `.harness/config/review-scope.json` still has no `cli_driver` group, and `bin/sotp review files --scope cli_driver` reports `Unknown scope`. As a result, changes under `apps/cli-driver/**` will not dispatch to this briefing, so the new D2/D3/D4 driver rules are unreachable until the review-scope config adds a matching pattern and `briefing_file`.

`lib.rs` exposes the six new driver modules, but those module files are untracked and absent from `git diff`/the index. If this branch is submitted as-is, the tracked change leaves dead module references here and the future `cli-driver` crate flip will fail to find these modules. Add the six files to the change set or defer these `pub mod` lines until the files are tracked.

`format_review_summary_stub` does not preserve the existing `format_review_summary` render contract: the live helper includes `Inline comments: {count}` and an `Inline Comments:` section with each finding's location/body, but this staged helper only renders the review id, state, and body. When T021 wires `pr_review_cycle` to this helper, Codex review summaries with inline findings would stop showing the actionable comments, making non-zero PR reviews appear much less informative than today.
